mcqs.question: Which of the following best defines a process in an operating system?

options.text: A program in execution with its current state
A batch job submitted to the system

mcqs.explanation: A process is a program in execution, which includes the current values of program counter, registers, and variables. A program is a passive entity (static code on disk), while a process is an active entity (dynamic, in memory).

mcqs.question: What is the main difference between a program and a process?

options.text: Program is passive, process is active
Program uses more memory than process
Program is hardware, process is software

mcqs.explanation: A program is a passive entity (static code stored on disk), while a process is an active entity (program in execution). The process has dynamic characteristics including memory, CPU registers, and program counter.

mcqs.question: In the process state diagram, when a process is moved from Running state to Ready state, it is due to:
mcqs.explanation: In preemptive scheduling, the scheduler forcibly removes a running process from CPU when its time quantum expires, moving it to the Ready state. This allows other processes to get CPU time.

mcqs.question: Which data structure contains all information about a process in the operating system?
mcqs.explanation: Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure that stores all information about a process including process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management info, I/O status information, and accounting data.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of Process Control Block (PCB)?
mcqs.explanation: PCB contains: Process ID, Process State, Program Counter, CPU Registers, Memory Management info, I/O Status, Accounting info, etc. User password is not stored in PCB - it's part of user authentication system, not process management.

mcqs.question: A process in 'Waiting' state is also known as:
mcqs.explanation: Waiting state is also called Blocked state. In this state, the process is waiting for some event to occur, such as I/O completion, availability of a resource, or a signal from another process.

mcqs.question: What happens when a process creates a child process using fork?

options.text: Both parent and child execute from the same point
Only the child process gets a new address space
The parent process waits for child to complete

mcqs.explanation: The fork system call creates a new process (child) that is a copy of the parent. After fork, both processes execute from the same point (the return from fork), but with different return values - parent gets child's PID, child gets 0.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a process scheduling queue in a typical OS?
mcqs.explanation: The three main scheduling queues are: Job Queue (all processes entering system), Ready Queue (processes in memory, ready to execute), and Waiting Queue (processes waiting for I/O or events). There is no 'Execution Queue'.

mcqs.question: In a many-to-many threading model:

options.text: Each user-level thread maps to exactly one kernel thread
Multiple user-level threads map to fewer or equal number of kernel threads
Each user-level thread maps to multiple kernel threads
User threads are not mapped to kernel threads

mcqs.explanation: In the many-to-many model, multiple user-level threads are mapped to a pool of kernel threads. This allows the OS to schedule an appropriate number of kernel threads based on the system resources, providing better concurrency than one-to-one model.

mcqs.question: What is the primary advantage of kernel-level threads over user-level threads?

options.text: Kernel has knowledge of all threads, enabling better scheduling

mcqs.explanation: Kernel-level threads are managed by the OS kernel, so it has full knowledge of all threads. This allows the kernel to make better scheduling decisions and enables blocking system calls without blocking the entire process. However, context switching between kernel threads is slower than user threads.

mcqs.question: Which thread library provides POSIX Pthreads?
mcqs.explanation: POSIX Pthreads (Portable Operating System Interface for Unix) is defined by IEEE POSIX b and implemented as the pthread library. It's the standard thread library for Unix/Linux systems.

mcqs.question: When a process performs I/O operation, its state changes from Running to:
mcqs.explanation: When a process initiates an I/O operation, it cannot continue execution until the I/O completes. Therefore, it moves from Running state to Waiting (or Blocked) state. Once I/O completes, it moves back to Ready state.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a benefit of multithreading?
mcqs.explanation: Multithreading provides improved CPU utilization (multiple threads can run on multiple CPUs) and enhanced responsiveness (background tasks can run without blocking the main thread). It doesn't eliminate synchronization needs - in fact, it increases them due to shared resources.

mcqs.question: In the one-to-one threading model (like Windows and Linux):

options.text: Many user threads share one kernel thread
Each user thread has its own kernel thread
User threads are not mapped to kernel threads
Kernel threads run in user mode

mcqs.explanation: The one-to-one model maps each user-level thread to a corresponding kernel-level thread. This provides good concurrency but has a limitation that creating many user threads can burden the kernel (limited number of kernel threads).

mcqs.question: A process transition from New to Ready state means:

options.text: Process has been loaded into memory

mcqs.explanation: The New state represents a process being created. When the OS completes loading the process into memory and sets up the PCB, the process moves to Ready state - meaning it is now in memory and ready to execute when CPU becomes available.

mcqs.question: What is context switching?

options.text: Switching between user and kernel mode
Saving and restoring CPU state when switching processes
Allocating new memory to a process

mcqs.explanation: Context switching is the process of saving the state (registers, program counter, etc.) of a currently running process and restoring the state of another process. This allows multiple processes to share a single CPU. It involves PCB operations and is a pure overhead.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an example of process creation?

options.text: Opening a file in an application

mcqs.explanation: Running a new program (like 'notepad' from command prompt) creates a new process. The parent process (command prompt) spawns a child process (notepad). Process creation involves allocating memory, creating PCB, and setting up address space.

mcqs.question: The 'zombie' process state occurs when:

options.text: Process has terminated but parent hasn't read its exit status
Process has been swapped to disk

mcqs.explanation: A zombie process is a terminated process whose parent hasn't yet read its exit status (using wait/waitpid). Until the parent reads the exit status, the process entry remains in the process table, consuming process ID.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the 'exec' system call family?

options.text: To replace the current process image with a new program

mcqs.explanation: The exec family replaces the current process image with a new program. It doesn't create a new process; instead, it loads a new program into the current process's address space. Often used with fork to create a child that runs a different program.

mcqs.question: In the many-to-one threading model, if one user thread blocks on I/O:

options.text: All user threads in the process block
The OS switches to another process

mcqs.explanation: In many-to-one model (user-level threads), all threads map to single kernel thread. Since the kernel sees only one thread, if any user thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks. This is a major limitation of many-to-one model.
